title: Navy Releases Fiscal 2026 Inactivation List Including Replenishment Oiler USNS John Ericsson
summary: U.S. Navy releases administrative message naming 14 ships for inactivation this fiscal year. Designates USNS John Ericsson as logistics support asset for cannibalization. Transfers multiple oilers and cargo ships to Maritime Administration.
contents:
## Navy Releases Fiscal 2026 Inactivation List Including Replenishment Oiler USNS John Ericsson

### Overview
U.S. Navy releases administrative message naming 14 ships for inactivation this fiscal year. Designates USNS John Ericsson as logistics support asset for cannibalization. Transfers multiple oilers and cargo ships to Maritime Administration.

### Event Details
U.S. Navy releases administrative message on April 27, 2026, detailing inactivation plans for 14 ships during fiscal 2026. Navy targets Ticonderoga-class guided-missile cruisers USS Shiloh and USS Lake Erie, Whidbey Island-class dock landing ship USS Germantown, and Henry J. Kaiser-class underway replenishment oiler USNS John Ericsson for conversion into logistics support assets. Navy employs these assets primarily for cannibalization and equipment removal to sustain active fleet operations. Navy schedules Freedom-class littoral combat ship USS Fort Worth for dismantling. Navy plans transfers of Watson-class vehicle cargo ships USNS Red Cloud, USNS Watkins, and USNS Pomeroy; Offshore Petroleum Distribution System vessel USNS Vadm. K. R. Wheeler; and Henry J. Kaiser-class underway replenishment oilers USNS Pecos and USNS Big Horn to U.S. Maritime Administration for disposal oversight. Military Sealift Command integrates lessons from prior operations, such as Iran conflict, into logistics strategies. This move streamlines fleet maintenance, optimizes resources, and supports Navy readiness amid evolving global demands. Navy administrative message outlines precise timelines and methods, ensuring efficient execution of inactivation process. Logistics support assets like USNS John Ericsson enhance sustainment capabilities for underway replenishment missions. Navy continues fleet modernization through strategic divestitures and reallocations.[1]

### Source Context
@MSCSealift (Military Sealift Command) covers this category of developments. The account focuses on usns military sealift command hospital ship deployment replenishment navy logistics.
